Dear Computer Science person,

     This letter is from The Molecular Sciences Institute.  We're looking to recruit a few computer science fellows to help with ongoing work in computational biology and bioinformatics.

      About the Institute.  It's a new (this February), non-profit research institute.  Its mission is to speed the development of genomic technologies and analytical and computational frameworks to enable prediction of the behavior of biological systems.  We believe that the predictive biology we want cannot be achieved without major contributions from computer science.  We further believe that this work should be overseen by biologists who know what the problems are and are willing to educate the computer scientists in
them.  We are making progress in learning how to bridge the considerable knowledge and culture gaps between mainstream biology and programming.

      So far, the Institute seems to be doing well.  There are now about 25 people here (20 researchers); by the end of 1999, there will probably be 35 or 40.  The existing cadre of research fellows is superb.  The majority of the people here are molecular biologists working on focused problems or on new genomic technologies.  The integration of computationalists and physicists into this work is intellectually and sociologically challenging.  Boundaries between the biology labs (at the moment, mine and Sydney Brenner's) are blurring, and the Institute has already emerged as a high-independence research and training environment.  Relations with UCSF and UC Berkeley are deepening nicely.  The lab space is lovely.  We plan to move from Berkeley to the UCSF Mission Bay development in 2002.

      Although our dream CS fellows would come with a number of attributes (see below), the bottom line is that we are looking for the brightest and most capable people, and we are committed to educating them.

       At the Institute, we are distinguishing between computational biology and bioinformatics.  Although this is kind of a gray distinction it is one that is important to make.  By computational biology we mean, above all, the use of computers to simulate and eventually predict the behaviors of living systems.  We believe that people who succeed in such work may make the most significant contributions to the biology of the early 21st century.  By bioinformatics, we mean the manipulation, analysis, and addition of value to mass-produced data from the genome projects and their follow-ons.  Such work is rife with unsolved problems, and each new data type creates more.  Even now, people who are good at it can write their own tickets in industry and are sought after in universities.  Work here covers both areas, and we are looking for both kinds of researchers.

Computational biology fellows might possess some of the following attributes:

      A rigorous undergraduate degree, and either meaningful graduate work (a PhD?) in mathematics, statistics, or computer science, or significant real world programming experience.

     Experience with "alternative computing" such as  neural nets, genetic algorithms, logic programming, etc., particularly if this experience had been applied to interesting, real-world problems.

     Experience in data analysis and visualization.

     Experience with simulation, preferably including implementation.

      A background in statistics, numerical analysis or other mathematics complementary to the existing backgrounds here (geometry).

Bioinformatics fellows might also possess:

      Experience with relational databases and administration thereof.

The fellows in charge of the databases might not need such a strong academic background, or as much programming experience, as the computational biologists.

      For both computational biology fellows and bioinformatics fellows, a thorough undergraduate-level background in contemporary molecular and cell biology would be a plus.

I've enclosed a list of ongoing research projects.  I hope you might post this and urge people you think appropriate to apply.
 
 

Best Regards,

Roger Brent
 
 
 

Ongoing and contemplated research areas at TMSI
 

Assays for protein interactions in vivo.

Interactions among evolutionarily conserved protein motifs.

Protein based recognition molecules.

Biological and physical probes for cellular events.

Functional genomic instrumentation.

Computational evolutionary biology.

Mathematical frameworks for simulation of cell function.

Directed selection and quantification of fitness in populations of evolving model organisms.

Computational tools to extract functional data from genomic information.
